nativeRange.habitat: Subtropical South American riverbanks, wetlands, and seasonally flooded floodplains

summary: A large South American rosette sword prized for its bright red-to-copper new growth that matures to deep green; an undemanding background or solitary centerpiece plant for medium-to-large planted tanks.
careNotes: Echinodorus osiris is a classic large Echinodorus from subtropical South America (originally described from the Uruguay drainage; molecular work has reassigned the species to the genus Aquarius as Aquarius uruguayensis, though the trade still universally uses 'Echinodorus osiris'). Care is essentially the same as the Amazon Sword (fw-plant-011) and other large Echinodorus: a heavy ROOT FEEDER that needs a deep, nutrient-rich substrate and regular root-tab replenishment. Reported adult size is 20–50 cm (Aqua-Fish.net), with the ribbon-shaped submerged leaves often reaching 20–30 cm long and 1–3 cm wide; under strong light and rich substrate, the upper end of 50 cm is achievable. Temperature 21–27 °C, pH 6.5–7.5, gH 4–18 are well tolerated. Light demand is medium — the plant grows under low light but red/copper coloration on new growth is markedly stronger under moderate-to-bright light. CO2 is not required (Modern Aquarium calls it 'undemanding'; Aqua-Fish.net notes CO2 is optional) but supplementation boosts leaf size and density. The signature feature is the COLOR PROGRESSION: new leaves emerge bright red, copper, or dark red-brown and gradually mature to green; iron supplementation (and good lighting) noticeably intensifies and prolongs the red phase. Place as a background or solitary centerpiece plant; in smaller tanks it quickly outgrows the layout. As with all Echinodorus, newly purchased plants often arrive with broader emersed-form leaves that melt back as submerged ribbon-shaped leaves replace them — this is normal.
breedingNotes: Propagates primarily by runners (stolons) that produce adventitious plantlets, plus daughter plants emerging around the mother rosette; once each plantlet has its own small rosette and visible roots it can be cut free and planted in the substrate. Mature plants can also be divided at the crown. Flowering and seed production occur readily under emersed/humid conditions (white corollas ~1–1.5 cm with ~18 stamens on racemose inflorescences) but are uncommon in fully submerged aquarium setups.

primary.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Echinodorus osiris
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters and care: size 20–50 cm, temperature 21–27 °C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 4–18 dGH, medium light (more red under brighter light), CO2 optional, fast growth, runners/adventitious plantlets, nutrient-rich substrate with root tabs, middle-to-background placement, native to Brazil/South America.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked moderate light, temperature 70–82 °F (~21–28 °C), midground-to-background placement, undemanding care level, heavy root feeder benefiting from iron-rich substrate and root caps, iron fertilization enhances leaf size and red coloration. Notes pairing with discus and angelfish (which may lay eggs on the broad leaves).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Echinodorus osiris
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Alismataceae, order Alismatales) and native range (Brazil, Argentina, Paraguay, Uruguay, Chile). Notes the modern reassignment to Aquarius uruguayensis, the two leaf forms (submersed ribbon-shaped 5–30–50 cm; emersed ovate 20–50 cm), red-brown undulate margins on submerged leaves, racemose inflorescences with ~18-stamen white flowers, and the characteristic reddish young growth.
